featuredImageAlt: The homepage Weekend Preview hero featuring CeeDee Lamb's headshot composited over a Cowboys-blue gradient, with a 'One to Watch · WR · DAL' caption

changes.summary: Team logos now dress for the dark. Marks with black outlines — the Raiders shield, the Steelers hypocycloids, the Jets, North Carolina's navy interlock — used to all but vanish against a dark background, a smudge where a logo should be. In dark mode every NFL logo across the site (matchup heroes, the composite banners, roster player rows) now swaps to its dark-optimized cut, outline flipped to white so the crest actually reads, and the college logos in the Free Agents and roster player tables get the same treatment. Light mode is untouched, and the switch follows your theme toggle in real time.

On phones, the homepage heroes — the What's New announcement and the red Breaking News banner — no longer type their headlines directly across the featured player's face. The text now keeps to its own column on the left, and the player gets the respect he deserves: anchored to the bottom-right corner, standing nearly the full height of the card, with his left edge fading softly into the gradient instead of ending in a hard rectangle behind the shoulders. Even on the narrowest screens, the two stay out of each other's way.

Player pop-ups got the hero treatment. Click any player on the rosters page and the modal now opens with a mini composite banner — his team's colors as the gradient, a ghost "QB RAVENS" wordmark behind him, and his cutout headshot bleeding off the edge, same visual language as the homepage heroes but at pop-up size. The injury pop-up goes a shade darker: the team color blends toward alert-red and the injury status itself looms behind the name. Defenses and players without a clean cutout get the gradient-and-wordmark treatment on its own, which still beats the old plain white header by a mile.

The homepage playoff heroes now wear their team's actual colors. The semifinal panels, the championship stage, and the wild-card glow used to tint from a chart color picked for looking distinct on a bar graph — fine for a spreadsheet, a little random on a hero. Every franchise now has a hand-tuned brand primary (pulled from its real logo and crest), so the Pigskins panel runs deep red, Bring The Pain goes black, Midwestside glows Hawkeye gold, and the title-game field melts from one team's color through black into the other's. Same crests, same layout — just the right paint.

Event windows and day boundaries across the site now flip on Pacific Time, not the server's clock. The production server lives in UTC, so "active now" banners lit up around 2 AM, deadlines read "Completed" by early afternoon on deadline day, and "Today" became "Tomorrow" at 5 PM. The server has been told what time zone the league actually lives in.

Event countdowns on the homepage What's Next cards and the League Calendar now count real calendar days. Events with a set time (deadlines, drafts) were shown as "1 day out" all morning on the day they actually happen — and occasionally "2 days out" for tomorrow's event. Day-of events now say "Today," which is when you most need them to.

The conference-draft pills on the AFL homepage now tell the truth on draft weekend. Once the American League's Saturday draft wrapped, its pill quietly skipped ahead to NEXT year's date ("Sat, Aug 28" — a Friday in 2026, which raised some eyebrows). Both pills also claimed the drafts start at 12:00 AM; they now show the real 9:00 AM PT start time everywhere, including the calendar.

The trade deadline countdown hero no longer risks flashing wrong numbers when the page loads. The countdown briefly showed the server's idea of the time remaining before the browser corrected it — on deadline day the digits could visibly disagree for a beat (and in date-testing mode the clock read a surreal 3,265 hours). It now waits for the browser clock and counts down cleanly from the first tick.

Feature announcements in the homepage hero now send you somewhere useful — the button links straight to the full article about that feature. Previously, announcements without a destination page pointed at the What's New listing (or, in one memorable case, at the very homepage you were already standing on).

The Lost Archives announcement in the homepage hero now stars what it's actually about: the recovered vintage Mistakes Were Made logo button, captioned "Circa 2007 · Recovered," instead of a rookie who wasn't even born when that logo was drawn. Announcements can now bring their own artwork whenever an image is the story.

Claude Schefter's homepage article heroes — the waiver report and the Friday weekend preview — now put a face to the story. The hero features whichever player the article actually leans on (the biggest waiver bid, the marquee projected starter), composited over his real NFL team's colors, instead of a generic card. If a player's photo is missing, his team logo stands in rather than an empty frame. Same treatment in light and dark mode.

The roster page's team switcher (and the team icons on Salary Analytics, the Cap Calculator, and the footer's defending-champion medallion) now show each club's current logo. Teams with a former identity — the Pigskins, Bring The Pain, Midwestside Connection, and the Dark Magicians of Chaos — were flying their retired 2007-era circle logo because the icon list was read oldest-first; it now skips the archived art and lands on the live logo.

The Free Agents page hero now spotlights whoever sits atop your current view — a broadcast-style panel with the player's ESPN headshot over their NFL team logo, updating live as you switch position tabs or re-sort (flip to QB and the panel becomes the top quarterback). The panel is styled for each theme — a light backdrop in light mode, a dark broadcast panel in dark mode — with the player's name on a label that stays readable over any jersey or logo, and the position filter pills now have a frosted-glass finish. The old rotating stock photos are gone, and the "See projected FAs" link is now a proper button you can actually see against the panel.

The Free Agents hero spotlight now puts a face on team defenses. Land on the DEF tab and instead of a lonely team crest, the panel shows that unit's marquee defenders — Maxx Crosby for the Raiders, T.J. Watt for the Steelers, Micah Parsons for Green Bay — their ESPN headshot front and center with the team logo faded behind as a watermark, exactly like the offensive-player treatment. Each defense carries a rotating cast of its top six defenders — Pro Bowlers and All-Pros are pinned to the front, with the rest ranked by real production so pass-rushers and ballhawks lead — and the whole roster refreshes itself weekly from live NFL rosters, so when a star changes teams his new crest is waiting for him. Teams without a clear face fall back to the clean logo-only look.

On phones, the Owner Activity page no longer stretches wider than the screen — the transaction and last-seen tables now scroll within their own lane, and the navigation drawer no longer peeks out along the right edge of the page.

The Former Identities strip on the Franchise History page no longer sends you on links to nowhere. Identities that belonged to a prior owner of a franchise slot (Mistakes Were Made, Devil Dogs, and friends) used to link to an era section that doesn't exist on the current owner's page — they now take you to that identity's vintage art in the Asset Library instead, and identities from a current owner's tenure still jump straight to their era.

The AFL Franchises page now has a Former Identities archive — all 94 names these clubs have played under since 2003, punitive last-place rebrands included, each with its restored vintage icon and a link to that franchise's name history. Tucked into a collapsible section so the page doesn't become a scroll marathon.

The Potential Targets card on the League Planner no longer shows up two, three, or even four times in a row for teams with a storied rebrand history — the planner was quietly rendering one copy per former team identity (Poker in the Rear and the Georgia Punishers don't need free agent advice anymore). Exactly one copy now renders for every team at every screen size.

The 2026 Rookie Rankings card in the page directory and homepage quick links has its icon back — it had been pointing at an icon that doesn't exist and rendering blank. Every icon in the directory is now checked against the real icon set automatically, so a missing icon can't quietly ship again.
The Constitution card in the page directory has its icon back (a gavel, matching the other rules pages) — it had been pointing at an icon that doesn't exist and rendering blank.

A handful of pages had been quietly haunted by the league's past lives — the Salary Analytics player tables credited players to retired identities like Under Siege and Devil Dogs instead of their current franchises, League Comparison and the draft capital table double-counted historical rebrands, and the roster page's draft cards could pull a vintage banner. Every franchise lookup now resolves to the team's current identity; the ghosts stay in the Asset Library where they belong.

Your saved team preference now always resolves to your club's current identity. With 82 retired team names in the AFL archives sharing franchise numbers with today's clubs, the preference system could quietly look up your conference and league placement against a name you rebranded away from years ago — it now only consults the current 24.

Franchise milestone posts in the Schefter Report now say what they're announcing — a labeled badge chip (like "🎯 Career Milestone · Playoff Veteran") sits above the one-liner, and every milestone post links straight to that team's badge wall. Previously you got a cryptic quip like "Not a tourist anymore" and were left to guess what happened.

The League's Asset Library now matches the AFL's organization — division badges live in their own Divisions section under league assets instead of masquerading as team cards in the alphabetical team grid.

The homepage hero and event cards (What's Next and the full League Calendar) now come in a proper light-mode outfit — clean white editorial cards keyed to each event's accent color — while the signature deep-navy chrome is reserved for dark mode. Same cards, correct wardrobe for the lighting.

The Import Rankings pop-ups (import details and delete confirmation) now dim the page behind them with the site's signature frosted-glass blur instead of a plain dark shade — matching the site's modal standard.

The delete-confirmation dialog in the Mock Draft lobby now gets the site's signature frosted-glass backdrop — the page behind it softly blurs instead of just dimming, matching every other modal on the site.

Owner Activity page content now lines up flush with the header and logo above it — previously the page was noticeably narrower and off-center from the nav bar, at every screen size from phone to desktop.
The AFL Icon Gallery was flat-out refusing to load (a wrong turn in how the page located the icon sprite file). It now opens like it always should have, with all 100+ icons browsable and searchable.

AFL accent colors across seven pages (Keepers, About, Calendar, Rules, Ask Roger, and both Franchises pages) now actually render AFL red — buttons, badges, stat highlights, and border accents had been quietly wearing The League's blue for years due to a color-token mix-up. The red was always in the code; it just never got its turn on screen.

The matchup data debug page no longer crashes with a server error during the off-season — team projection math now handles MFL's empty off-season projections feed gracefully (and any page using team projections is protected from the same quirk).

The Asset Library's title banner is readable again — it had been rendering white text on a near-white background, making "The League Asset Library" heading effectively invisible. The banner now wears the dark slate look it was always meant to have.

Franchise history and career records were quietly undercounting years — 2007-2018 was missing week 17 entirely, several seasons between 2019-2024 were missing several weeks outright, and even recent years could drop a team's score when its game didn't fit the usual matchup format. All 2007-2025 seasons now carry every week's real scores, so highest-game and biggest-blowout records reflect what actually happened (turns out someone had a bigger week than anyone knew about).

The AFL playoffs page now shows era-correct team names and icons when browsing past seasons — flipping back to 2013 gets you The Nukes, booyakasha, and The Dude that Abides with their vintage artwork, instead of every bracket wearing this year's rebrands.

The League's division standings finally look the part. Each division table is now topped by a branded blue banner: the division's own compass badge on the left (Northwest, Southwest, Central, East each get their own mark), the division name in big condensed caps, and the defending champion's logo riding shotgun on a white medallion right beside it. The old plain-text "Division Champions: …" line is gone — the hardware speaks for itself now.

The AFL team The Show has fresh artwork — a new graffiti-style avatar now shows up wherever the team appears across the site (standings, rosters, and the GroupMe chat), replacing the old placeholder mark.

The League's homepage hero and What's New page no longer pick up AFL-only announcements — an untagged AFL feature (the franchise trophy room) had been headlining The League's homepage. League tagging is now mandatory and fails closed, so cross-league mix-ups can't happen again.

Historical standings pages now show every team's real banner from that season — nine or more rows per year had broken images pointing at the league's long-dead old websites. Vintage banners for the Degenerates, Las Vegas Elite, Devil Dogs, Executioners, Sabertooths, and a dozen more were recovered from the Internet Archive and the league's old MFL records.

The 2007, 2010, 2011, and 2015 season records were quietly wrong — those years had been synced from other leagues that happened to share an MFL league ID. Standings, schedules, playoff brackets, and weekly scores for those seasons now reflect The League's actual history (2015 has all 16 teams again).

Every AFL franchise's full name history is now on the books — 94 legacy identities across 22 teams (from Alcohol, Tobacco and Firearms to all thirteen of franchise 0016's rebrands), each with its authentic banner art recovered from the old afl-fantasy.com site and internet archives. Historical seasons now show the team names and banners as they actually were.

Historical team identities now have proper circular icons, cropped from the best part of each vintage banner (mascots and emblems over wordmarks), and the Asset Library is reorganized into Active Teams, Former Teams, Championship Art, League Logos, Conferences, and Divisions — with a card for every legacy identity showing its banner, icon, and the years (and conference) it was active.

The AFL Asset Library now has a card for every legacy team identity, reorganized into Active Teams, Former Teams, Championship Art, League Logos, Conferences, and Divisions — each showing its banner, circular icon, and the years (and conference) it was active.
Chatmaster's GroupMe avatar was a stretched copy of the team banner — it's now a proper square avatar featuring the classic AIM running man.

17 AFL seasons (2007-2023) were quietly wrong — those years had been synced from other leagues that happened to share an MFL league ID (2015-2023 were literally showing The League's data instead of AFL's). Standings, schedules, transactions, drafts, and playoff brackets for all 17 seasons now reflect AFL's actual history.

The 2007-2017 stretch of Dead Cap Walking's history had been split into two mismatched records — a 2017 entry wrongly showed The Art of War's artwork under the Las Vegas Elite name. The League's own records confirm 2007-2017 was continuously Las Vegas Elite; that era now shows its real banner and icon, with The Art of War's brief 2018 run as its own distinct entry.

Cleaned up a handful of duplicate and mismatched icons in the Asset Library — a few AFL teams (Chatmaster, Computer Jocks, Da Dangsters) were showing an extra auto-generated icon alongside their real one, and the League's crest logo was rendering oversized in the League Logos section.

Long file names in the Asset Library no longer spill out of their cards — they now truncate cleanly with an ellipsis, and every asset card has one-click copy buttons for both the file name and the full URL (clicking the URL field also selects the whole thing).

Historical AFL Premier League / D-League standings (2020-2024) now show the correct tier rosters. The all-play view was grouping past seasons by each team's CURRENT tier, so promoted and relegated teams appeared in the wrong league for old years — the official per-season tier makeups were recovered from the league's MFL skin and the standings page now matches MFL's own historical tables team-for-team.

In the Keeper Planner, the "send back to cut pool" arrows on your keepers now match the polished icon-button look of the "keep" arrows below the line — they had been rendering as bare unstyled browser buttons.

AFL historical standings now show each team's name and icon as they were that season — viewing 2013 shows The Nukes, booyakasha, and The Dude that Abides with their vintage icons instead of every year wearing today's branding. Names and icons are era-correct across all three views for every season back to 2003, and the my-team highlight follows owners across the 2016-17 conference realignment slot swaps.

AFL historical standings now use each season's real division layout. The 2003-2012 seasons ran six divisions (AL North/Central/South, NL East/West/Pacific) with four teams each, but the page had been cramming every year into today's four divisions — inventing division winners, seeds, and playoff fields that never existed. Every season back to 2003 now groups teams into its actual divisions with the right winners and seeds (verified against the trophy room's award history), the playoff view seeds three division winners per conference in the six-division era, and seasons that predate all-play tracking show a dash instead of a fabricated 0-240 record.

The All-Play view on historical AFL standings now matches the competition's real history. 2017 — the inaugural season, the year after the last AFL Cup, which ran as one 24-team table — shows that single unbranded table, and the 2018 and 2019 Premier League / D-League rosters were recovered from the league's own website scripts (the official source for who played in which league), so those seasons now show their true two-table split. Seasons before 2017 no longer offer an All-Play tab at all. Along the way, four seasons of missing weekly scores (2016-2019 regular seasons, invisible because MFL's archive uses an older data format) were recovered, so every all-play record shown is a real number — and the recovered 2018/2019 tables were verified by reproducing the known champions of both leagues in both years.

Corrected the 2025 Premier League roster: No Soup For You played in the Premier League and Suh girls, one cup in the D-League — the site had them swapped (the league website's official groupings say otherwise, and they win). The 2026 makeup was recalculated from the corrected base, so this year's Premier League and D-League rosters shown around the site now match where teams will actually play.

2017's one-table all-play season has a name now: the Founders Table — the year that decided who started in the Premier League and who started in the D-League when the split launched in 2018. It still has no Premier League branding (there wasn't one yet), but the promotion line is back after the 12th-place team, exactly where it belonged, with a green arrow marking every team that moved on to the inaugural Premier League.

AFL standings now get the same glowing glass treatment as The League — every division, conference, and tier card lights up in its own color, with the National League going blue and the American League red so you can tell the conferences apart at a glance. Your own team's row glows to match its card.

The hamburger menu button in the top bar dropped its glossy background plate — no more lit-up pill sitting behind it in dark mode, and no more highlight that stuck around after a tap on mobile. Three clean lines, nothing else. The same cleanup went out to the forum's embedded nav, so the button looks identical everywhere you meet it.
